title: SEGMENT 16 — The competitive landscape: MGI, Element, Ultima

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — The competitive landscape: MGI, Element, Ultima
For most of the 2010s, Illumina was effectively without serious competition in short-read sequencing.

The early NGS competitors — 454 Life Sciences (pyrosequencing), Applied Biosystems SOLiD (sequencing by ligation), Helicos (single-molecule sequencing without amplification) — had all either been acquired and discontinued, or had simply failed in the market.
Illumina was the platform.
That has been changing in the last few years, as patents on the core Solexa chemistry have expired and new entrants have brought competing technologies to market.
Let me walk you through the current competitive landscape.
MGI Tech.
MGI is a subsidiary of BGI — the Beijing Genomics Institute, one of the world's largest sequencing centers.

After years of using Illumina instruments, BGI acquired Complete Genomics, a US sequencing company with its own platform, in 2013, and developed it into a competing short-read sequencer that BGI now sells globally as MGI.
The MGI platform — called DNBSEQ — uses DNA nanoballs instead of bridge amplification.

Each starting fragment is amplified by rolling-circle replication into a long concatemer that condenses into a tight ball about 200 nanometers across, and these nanoballs are deposited onto patterned flow cells.
The sequencing chemistry is similar to Illumina — fluorescent nucleotides, cyclic incorporation and imaging — though with some chemistry differences.
MGI claims slightly lower per-base costs than Illumina and has been gaining share, particularly in Asia and Europe.
The platform's data is broadly compatible with Illumina-style analysis pipelines, and many studies have found the data quality is comparable for most applications.
Element Biosciences.
A US startup founded by former Illumina engineers, Element launched its AVITI platform in 2022.

AVITI uses an interesting twist on the SBS chemistry called avidity sequencing: instead of fluorescent dyes attached directly to individual nucleotides, the system uses unlabeled nucleotides for the actual chain extension, followed by separate detection with multivalent fluorescent probes that bind specifically to the newly incorporated base.
This decouples the incorporation chemistry from the imaging chemistry and apparently enables higher per-base accuracy and lower reagent costs.
Element targets the mid-throughput market — somewhat similar to Illumina's NextSeq — and has been growing.
Ultima Genomics.
Another startup that emerged from stealth in 2022 with a quite different approach.
Ultima uses a circular wafer that spins like a hard disk during sequencing, with the chemistry happening at the spinning surface.
The sequencing chemistry uses unblocked nucleotides — no reversible terminators — and instead controls incorporation by limiting the local availability of nucleotides.
This gives them somewhat longer reads in some applications and very high throughput.
Ultima has positioned itself as a high-volume, low-cost-per-base platform, with claims approaching the $100 genome.
What do all these short-read competitors have in common?
They share the fundamental design: fragments amplified into clonal clusters or balls on a surface, fluorescent detection after each base, parallel imaging of billions of features simultaneously.
The Solexa architecture — clusters, cyclic chemistry, parallel imaging — has become the universal pattern for short-read sequencing.
The competition is on details: chemistry efficiency, optical engineering, cost structure, throughput per run, error profile.
But the basic shape of the platform is the same.
In a sense, Illumina's dominance shaped the entire competitive landscape — the competitors all look like variations on the Illumina theme.
The competitors are real, but Illumina's lead is still substantial.

Illumina has the largest installed base of instruments, the deepest set of regulatory approvals for clinical use, the most extensive partnerships with library prep companies and analysis tools, and the largest engineering organization.

Competition has driven prices down — the NovaSeq X launch was partly in response to competitive pressure — but Illumina's market share, while no longer at the peak of 90+ percent, is still dominant at roughly 70 to 80 percent of the NGS market.
Long-read platforms — PacBio and Oxford Nanopore.
These are technically not direct competitors to Illumina, because they solve a different problem: reading much longer fragments at the cost of higher per-base error.

Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) uses a real-time single-molecule sequencing-by-synthesis approach with a different chemistry, producing reads typically 10,000 to 30,000 base pairs long with current accuracy near Illumina's after circular consensus correction.

Oxford Nanopore uses a completely different paradigm — reading DNA by threading it through a protein nanopore and measuring changes in electrical current — producing reads of up to several million base pairs, though with higher raw error rates than Illumina or PacBio.

Long-read platforms shine where Illumina struggles: spanning long repeats, de novo assembly of complex genomes, characterizing structural variants, detecting DNA modifications like methylation directly without bisulfite conversion.

Many modern genome projects use a hybrid approach: Illumina for the bulk of the data (cost-effective, accurate at the base level) plus a smaller amount of long-read data (for the parts that Illumina can't resolve).
The two technologies are complementary more than competitive, and the field is converging on multi-platform analyses as standard for high-quality genome work.
Where this whole landscape goes in the next decade is unclear.
Long-read costs are dropping fast — Nanopore in particular keeps cutting prices on its small instruments — and at some point the cost-versus-accuracy tradeoff may shift.

Whether short-read sequencing continues to dominate as a routine assay, or whether long-read becomes cheap enough and accurate enough to displace it for some applications, is one of the big open questions in the field.
